briefing.opening: Good morning. We will now begin the climate briefing for May 22, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Climate Crisis and Immediate Threats
sections.content: The acceleration of climate change is emerging as a clear threat in various parts of the globe. Domestically, the risk of snakebites is projected to increase by up to 50% by 2090 due to climate change, and the conservation of 'Pungnyeolji' (wind caves), a biological refuge for 82 species of rare northern plants, is an urgent matter [3, 4, 7, 9]. Internationally, abnormal signs in ecosystems continue to be observed, such as roses at Chartwell House in the UK blooming at their earliest recorded time, and the 'Doomsday Glacier' facing difficulties [76, 86]. Furthermore, warnings suggest that the climate crisis could escalate into a security threat, with rivers worldwide quietly suffocating and agriculture being scorched by the flames of climate change [85, 91, 88]. In this context, moves by certain countries to relax regulations on 'super-warming substances' or to abolish climate change response ordinances raise further concerns, as they could lead to a serious setback in international climate action efforts [10, 78, 90].

sections.title: 🌍 Strengthening International Legal Obligations for Climate Action and Policy Trends
sections.content: Responding to climate change is increasingly becoming a firm international legal obligation. A recommendatory resolution adopted by the International Court of Justice (ICJ) explicitly states that climate action is a legal obligation, and UN member states have also agreed on legal obligations for climate change response [2, 82]. This aligns with movements encouraging practical action, such as the Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) shifting the focus of climate targets from 'setting' to 'implementation' [6]. However, despite these international efforts, the passive stance of some countries, such as the United States opposing the UN vote to affirm responsibility for climate change mitigation, remains a persistent challenge [80].

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Carbon Neutrality Transition by Domestic Local Governments and Private Sector
sections.content: Domestically, local governments and the private sector are actively undertaking concrete efforts to achieve carbon neutrality and adapt to climate change. Gwangju City has shown significant achievements, recording a 94% target achievement rate and reducing 310,000 tons of greenhouse gases through 154 carbon-neutral projects with an investment of 581.5 billion KRW [16, 17, 27, 31, 33, 36, 37, 38, 43, 44, 46]. Furthermore, various organizations, including the Korea Chamber of Commerce and Industry and Korea Southern Power, Ansan Green Environment Center and Shinansan University, Konkuk University and Korea Western Power, and the Korean Red Cross and Net Zero Climate Foundation, are expanding their scope of cooperation by signing MOUs to implement carbon neutrality and promote energy transition [15, 20, 24, 25, 32, 40, 41, 45, 48, 74]. Citizen participation and regionally specialized efforts are also prominent, such as Nowon-gu's youth laboratory in cooperation with Greenpeace, Dobong-gu's Carbon Empathy Mileage exceeding 5 million participations, and Gurye-gun's preparation for a 'Soil Revitalization Expo' for carbon-neutral agriculture. The case of the naturally restored Gounsa temple forest becoming more resilient to disasters after the Gyeongbuk wildfire demonstrates nature's recovery power [1, 8, 34, 35].

sections.title: 🌱 Expansion of Renewable Energy and Challenges of Energy Transition
sections.content: The expansion of renewable energy supply is establishing itself as a key driver for domestic energy transition. Gwangmyeong City and Siheung City are making efforts to expand supply by conducting demand surveys for renewable energy facility installations in homes and buildings and providing subsidies. Gyeongsangnam-do also hosted the nation's first 'Solar Renewable Energy Comprehensive Service Company (ReSCO) Market' [49, 58, 59, 64, 66, 67, 71]. Hanwha Q CELLS has demonstrated its technological prowess by supplying domestically produced cells and modules for the largest solar power project in Korea, and Korea Hydro & Nuclear Power is exploring leadership in the future energy market through the commercialization of clean hydrogen and the combination of nuclear and renewable energy [55, 65, 72]. However, challenges remain, such as criticisms that the enforcement decree on setback distances in the renewable energy basic plan does not align with the legislative intent, and the issue of regional electricity self-sufficiency rates differing by up to 729 times [50, 61]. As the government views grid expansion as a 'golden time' and accelerates the grand energy transition, it will need to focus on resolving these bottlenecks and disseminating public-led renewable energy models [51, 56, 68].

briefing.closing: As we have seen in today's briefing, the warnings of the climate crisis are becoming clearer, but at the same time, various domestic and international efforts and movements towards overcoming it are also accelerating. This is a time when sustained attention and action are becoming even more crucial. This concludes today's climate briefing.
